text: The surgeon attempts to separate the normal fatty tissue and vessels from the hernia sac at the internal ring, revealing a significant amount of surrounding fat. The leading edge of the hernia sac is not yet clearly identified.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac, revealing an extra piece of fat on the medial side of the cord structure. During medial dissection past the midline, a small vessel is torn, leading to a pooling of blood in the retropubic space.

Blood visibly pools in the retropubic space, increasing the risk of injury to the bladder, obturator vessels, nerve, and coronamortis. The surgeon attempts to control the bleeding using bipolar cautery while focusing on reaching a large piece of retroperitoneal fat.

The surgeon dissects down to the pubis, encountering fatty tissue that obscures clear anatomical planes. A large piece of medial vocal ligament fat is visible laterally, complicating the dissection. Additionally, a clump of fat is seen hanging off the epigastric vessel on the left side of the screen. The hernia sac appears to have been reduced.

The surgeon identifies a broad but shallow scrotal hernia sac and points out the separation between the testicular vessels and a medial piece of fat contributing to the hernia's size. The testicular vessels are visibly displaced laterally.

The surgeon cautiously navigates the surgical field, noting the potential variability in the location of the vas deferens relative to the testicular vessels. A small vessel continues to bleed slightly, running along the fat on the medial side of the space. The surgeon attempts to remove the dangling fat from the inferior gastric region.

The surgeon identifies a separate piece of fat emerging from the inferior gastric region, distinct from the lateral retroperitoneal fat. A clear plane is visible between these two fat deposits, and the dissection resembles the process of dissecting around the transverse salus sling, although it is not that structure.

The surgeon identifies vessels and lymphatics within the tissue, dividing them to facilitate complete reduction. The surgical field shows a pool of blood that has slowly accumulated, and the surgeon follows natural planes to prevent further bleeding.

The surgeon dissects two centimeters deep into the retropubic space to obtain the critical view of the myopectic ulcer, focusing on an area to the right of midline where there is no bleeding. The transfer salus sling is visible during the dissection.

The surgeon navigates around a pool of blood by dissecting through a clean plane to the right of the midline, preventing the blood from infiltrating the area. The dissection proceeds safely through the bloody tissue using this strategy.

The surgeon identifies a natural tissue plane, emphasizing the importance of hemostasis to clearly visualize the separation between the medial molecular fat and the vas deferens. The shiny fat to the right of the bleeding area is identified as the obturator nodes, which the surgeon aims to preserve while prioritizing the cord.

The surgeon assesses the distance of the vas deferens from the dissection plane and continues the procedure. A piece of gauze is inserted to aid in dissection near the medial and vocal ligament. The surgeon carefully peels fat away from the vas deferens and testicular vessel, preparing to insert a piece of mesh into the retropubic space. The mesh is positioned to cover the obturator nodes and the vas deferens, preventing fat from slipping underneath.

The surgeon exposes the obturator node and creates a small window to suture to Cooper's ligament. The wide obturator node packet is visible, and the medial umbilical ligament area is shown. A tubular structure is observed, but due to distorted anatomy, it is left alone as it may be the ureter.

The surgeon mobilizes the fat around a tubular structure without dividing it, applying a clip to secure divided lymphatics and vessels. The tubular structure is traced along the medial umbilical ligament fold, which lifts and tethers the fold, suggesting it is the medial umbilical ligament. The vas deferens is visible, positioned laterally due to the large medial umbilical ligament path.

The surgeon identifies a large mass of medial umbilical ligament fat, which is unusually positioned medially rather than laterally. This fat mass visibly displaces the testicular vessels, and there is noticeable bleeding from small vessels in the area.

The surgeon prepares to place a mesh over a large defect, visible alongside a lymph node. The assistant switches to a mega suture cut needle driver, while the surgeon clips a previously divided lymphatic channel.

The surgeon uses a Raytech to blot blood near the medial umbilical ligament. An extra-large 3D Max mesh is visible on the left side, already placed in the abdomen before docking the robot. The mesh covers the area, but additional work is needed to address the peritoneum on the testicular vessels.

The surgeon carefully tucks the mesh into place to prevent herniation of the fatty packet beneath it. A varicose suture is placed to Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh, ensuring it does not billow out through the large defect or lift at the lower edge.

The surgeon places additional sutures around the large defect to secure the mesh, ensuring it does not protrude through the defect. The mesh is positioned around the internal ring, with the option to placate or sew the transversalis fascia to Cooper's ligament for direct defects.

The surgeon pauses to take pictures, revealing the size of the reduced fat. The visual shows the significant amount of fat that was reduced, which was contributing to the palpable mass along with part of the colon acting as a slider in the internal ring.

The surgeon completes the final steps of the inguinal hernia repair by having the bedside assistant insert a suction cannula to rapidly desufflate the pocket, allowing CO₂ to escape. The surgeon attempts to perform two suture throws before the needle driver is introduced, managing only one throw.
